title: Optimal Mechanism Design with Referral
abstract: This paper establishes the optimal selling mechanism when a seller can incentivize an existing buyer to refer his privately known potential buyer to participate. We identify three optimal channels for providing referral incentives. First, if the existing buyer declares that no potential buyer exists, his virtual value is penalized. Second, if the existing buyer refers the potential buyer to the seller, his virtual value is boosted. Third, in some scenarios where this carrots-and-sticks-via-virtual-value approach is insufficient for creating proper referral incentives, the existing buyer is then given a constant referral bonus for referring the potential buyer. We also provide conditions under which the optimal mechanism can be implemented using simple mechanisms. Finally, we demonstrate that the conventional resale mechanism is suboptimal.

author.Hao Zhou: China Institute of Regulation Research, Zhejiang University of Finance and Economics, Hangzhou 310018, China
author.Jun Zhang: Economics Discipline Group, School of Business, University of Technology Sydney, Sydney, New South Wales 2007, Australia

article.1. Introduction: It has been well established in the literature that having more buyers benefits sellers. For instance, a famous result inBulow and Klemperer (1996)shows that attracting one more buyer is better than conducting optimal negotiation with an existing buyer. One way to attract more buyers is by asking existing buyers to refer potential buyers who may not be directly accessible to the seller. However, existing buyers may hesitate to recommend potential buyers, as such referrals could potentially lower their chances of obtaining the object. Additionally, in cases where they do secure the object, there may be concerns about paying a higher price. Therefore, existing buyers must be incentivized to refer other potential buyers. Indeed, providing referral incentives is a common practice adopted by many sellers in the real world. In the realm of private art sales, artists cannot only directly sell to existing collectors or enthusiasts they know, but also provide referral awards for them to invite other collectors within their own circles. The discourse surrounding referral incentives within the art industry has garnered substantial attention in both industry circles and the media. The article titled “Get people to sell your art for you” emphasized that one pivotal element in establishing a successful referral program is capitalizing on artists’ pre-existing connections with collectors: “You want affiliates to be people who genuinely love and support your art. Who better than your fans to rave about you and your art?”1The article titled “Should Artists Pay Referral Fees,” written by an art journalist Daniel Grant, mentioned that famous artists like Janet Fish “called one of her collectors to ask that person to contact a particular gallery about her work.”2 Nowadays, the emergence of advanced technology has further streamlined the referral process, facilitating an increasingly sophisticated ability to monitor “who refers whom” using referral links. Auction houses in many industries have embraced this trend by launching formal referral programs to replace informal agreements. For instance, Hessink’s Fine Art Auctioneers, established in 1993 and standing as one of the foremost auction houses in the Netherlands, introduce a buyer referrals program that offers a 20% commission if a buyer knows someone who is looking for an item that they happen to have on offer. Similarly, in used car auctions, Halifax Auction Direct in Canada offers a $200 check if buyers have friends, family, or colleagues who are in the market for a new or used car, truck, or Sport Utility Vehicle. In Initial Public Offering auctions, as pointed out inJeong and Lee (2024), sellers not only directly sell to institutional investors, such as venture capital and investment banks, but also provide them with commissions if individual investors purchase shares through them. The previous examples share the common feature that referring potential buyers could lower existing buyers’ chances of winning. This leads to challenges in designing optimal mechanisms when the seller relies on existing buyers to access potential buyers. Two fundamental questions arise. (1) Is it advantageous to incentivize existing buyers to refer potential buyers? (2) More importantly, if so, how can optimal referral incentives be designed? In this paper, we take the first step to address the challenge of designing optimal mechanisms in the presence of referral incentives. We consider a scenario where a seller has one unit of an indivisible object to sell and there is one existing buyer who privately knows whether another potential buyer exists. The seller can incentivize the existing buyer to refer the potential buyer to participate and compete for the object. We fully characterize the optimal mechanism, which involves three distinct channels for providing referral incentives. First, if the existing buyer claims that no potential buyer exists, his “virtual value” is penalized, effectively receiving a “stick” as a disincentive. Second, if the existing buyer successfully refers the potential buyer to the seller, his virtual value is boosted, akin to receiving a “carrot” as a reward. However, in cases where these carrots-and-sticks adjustments via virtual value fail to adequately incentivize the existing buyer, a constant referral bonus is provided for successfully referring the potential buyer. We refer to these three channels as virtual stick, virtual carrot, and referral bonus, respectively. To illustrate the main concept of the paper, consider the following simple example: An emerging artist named Alice wishes to sell an artwork and currently only knows one local collector. The local collector’s valuation of the artwork is uniformly distributed in the interval [0, 1]. The optimal mechanism in this case is straightforward. Alice simply sets a price of1/2for the local collector, resulting in expected revenue of1/4. Now, let us introduce a scenario where with probability1/4, the local collector knows a national collector who values the artwork at 2. However, Alice must rely on the local collector to reach the national collector, who is out of her immediate reach. If Alice ignores the potential buyer and maintains the previous mechanism, the local collector has no incentive to refer her to the national collector, leaving Alice’s expected revenue unchanged. However, by implementing a referral incentive mechanism, Alice can improve her revenue potential. Consider the following mechanism. Alice tells the local collector that “You can buy the artwork at2/3, or you can find someone and earn a commission of1/3dollars.” In this case, even the local collector with value 1 would like to refer Alice to the national collector. As a result, Alice’s expected revenue increases to7/12. It turns out that the above mechanism is actually the optimal mechanism. The example illustrates the three channels to provide referral incentives. Apparently, Alice explicitly provides a constant referral bonus of1/3if the local collector successfully refers the national collector. This corresponds to the channel of referral bonus. The other two channels are relatively implicit. First, when the local collector declares that there is no national collector, Alice adjusts the selling price higher to2/3compared with the baseline price of1/2. This increase in price serves as the virtue stick to diminish the local collector’s optional value from nonreferral, thereby incentivizing him to refer the potential buyer. Second, after the local collector refers to the national collector, when deciding whether to sell to the local collector or the national collector, Alice actually boosts the local collector’s virtual valuation. This virtue carrot is used to enhance the local collector’s payoff from referral. However, in this example, the national collector has a much higher value and even the boosted virtual valuation falls short. This example suggests that referral incentives are more prevalent than they may initially seem: In addition to the explicit incentives commonly provided, implicit incentives also exist. Although Daniel Grant’s article discusses the payment of referral fees, it primarily focuses on explicit incentives. His article should really be about “Should Artists PayExplicitReferral Fees.” However, as demonstrated in this example, implicit incentives also play a significant role in the optimal mechanism for the seller. In contrast to the standard optimal auction model inMyerson (1981), our paper introduces two novel elements. First, instead of the one-dimensional private information for buyers (i.e., their values for the item) in the standard model, we explore a scenario where the existing buyer’s private information is two dimensional: his value for the item and whether he knows a potential buyer. Second, in contrast to the fixed number of buyers in the standard model, the inclusion of an additional buyer is based on the referral made by an existing buyer. These deviations introduce an additional incentive compatibility constraint (or the referral constraint as sometimes we call it in the paper), particularly concerning the existing buyer’s potential misreporting of both his value and the existence of a potential buyer. Consequently, direct application of pointwise optimization techniques, as used in the standard model, becomes impractical. Our main technical innovation lies in recognizing that the incentive compatibility constraint regarding referral incentives is most stringent when the existing buyer’s value reaches its upper bound. Leveraging this insight, we relaxed the original problem by assuming the referral constraint is only satisfied at the existing buyer’s highest value, resulting in a simplified problem, which can be solved using continuous linear programming techniques. Remarkably, we demonstrate that the solution to this relaxed problem remains feasible (and therefore optimal) for the original problem. Through these techniques, we fully characterize the optimal mechanism, highlighting the concept of adjusted virtue valuation as pivotal in our scenario. Our optimal mechanism evaluates buyers based on their adjusted virtue valuations, reflecting the influence of referral incentives. Moreover, our model sometimes requires the inclusion of unconditional referral bonuses. Thus, our approach offers a nuanced understanding of optimal mechanism design in the presence of referral incentives, addressing the complexities introduced by multidimensional private information and variable buyer participation. We also conduct comparative static analysis. When the potential buyer is more likely to exist, the existing buyer receives more virtual sticks with nonreferral, less virtual carrots with referral and a lower referral bonus. The existing buyer is worse off regardless of his valuation. In contrast, when the potential buyer becomes “stronger,” the existing buyer receives more virtual sticks with nonreferral, more virtual carrots with referral, and a higher referral bonus. The existing buyer with the highest (lowest) valuation is worse (better) off. Finally, we investigate when the optimal mechanism can be implemented by simple mechanisms. With uniform distributions, the optimal mechanism can be implemented by a second-price auction with a reserve and a constant conditional coupon. This mechanism is simple and easy to use since the conditional coupon is a fixed number and independent of bids. Furthermore, when the valuation of the potential buyer is sufficiently high, the optimal mechanism aligns with sequential selling methods commonly adopted in practice. In this scenario, the seller initially presents a take-or-leave-it offer to the existing buyer. If declined, the seller offers a constant cash reward for referral to the existing buyer and then makes a take-it-or-leave-it offer to the potential buyer. Alice’s optimal mechanism serves as an example of sequential selling. One might question why the seller does not simply sell the object to the existing buyer, who could then resell it to the potential buyer. However, we demonstrate that this conventional resale mechanism is never optimal. Instead, it is always better for the seller to directly involve the potential buyer in the mechanism when he exists. In general, our optimal mechanism involves an auction between the existing buyer and the potential buyer. Despite the significance and relevance of referral in auctions, there is little investigation from the optimal mechanism design approach. Nevertheless, there exists an emerging literature studying auctions in social networks. The approach of this literature is quite different from ours. They allow a general network structure among buyers and assume that buyers can only communicate with neighbors. Their aim is to design a mechanism with good properties including full diffusion of information, strategy-proofness, individual rationality, and weakly budget-balance. In some sense, this literature studies the problem from an axiomatic approach as in the vast matching literature pioneered byGale and Shapley (1962)andShapley and Scarf (1974). A straightforward attempted mechanism is the VCG mechanism. However,Li et al. (2017)show that it satisfies all properties except weak budget balance, implying that the seller may not have an incentive to adopt it. Various mechanisms are then proposed to improve upon VCG mechanisms in terms ofex-postrevenue: information diffusion mechanism inLi et al. (2017), closest winner of Myerson’s and partial potential winners of Myerson's mechanisms inZhang et al. (2023), critical diffusion mechanism inLi et al. (2019), multilevel mechanism inLee (2017), and groupwise-pivotal referral mechanismJeong and Lee (2024).3Because the implementation of the mechanisms depends on the structure of the social network, another important question in this literature is the computation using algorithms, which makes this strand of literature more related to computer science. We take the traditional approach of mechanism design followingMyerson (1981).4Instead of looking for strategy-proof mechanisms to improve the ex post revenue, we are interested in the optimal Bayesian incentive compatible mechanism to maximize the seller’s ex ante revenue. To our knowledge, we are the first in the literature to study optimal referral incentives in auctions. It can be verified that none of the previous mechanisms maximizes the seller’s ex ante revenue in our simple framework. One obvious difference is that when the potential buyer does not exist, all these mechanisms allocate the object to the existing buyer in Myerson’s fashion, whereas the optimal mechanism needs to distort the allocation. Our paper is related to the literature on referral in marketplaces withBiyalogorsky et al. (2001)as one of the first articles to study the optimal reward structure for diffuse referrals. More recently,Lobel et al. (2017)study how to optimally attract new buyers by using a referral program. They find the optimal nonlinear referral payment function for the seller and argue that a linear referral payment program with a threshold bonus can approximately implement the optimal payment function.Leduc et al. (2017)study the case where the seller attracts part of the buyers to try a new product and then refers other potential buyers. They establish the condition under which the referral reward program is better than the intertemporal price discrimination. In these models, the seller sells multiple units of homogeneous products; therefore, the new buyer and the existing buyer do not have to compete after the referral. In contrast, in our paper, referring a new buyer induces more competition for existing buyers, which makes the referral incentive challenging to design. Technically, we examine the issue from the mechanism design approach that enables us to examine all possible referral incentives instead of assuming particular forms.5 Our paper builds on the vast literature on optimal mechanism design pioneered byMyerson (1981)and is related mainly to studies with multidimensional private information. Although the canonical one-dimensional mechanism design model is well understood, its natural multidimensional analogue turns out to be much less tractable.Rochet (1985)shows that a mechanism is incentive compatible if and only if the buyer’s utility induced by the mechanism is convex, as in one-dimensional environments.Laffont et al. (1987)consider a model with a single good, but where consumers are differentiated by a two-dimensional parameter.McAfee and McMillan (1988)extendsLaffont et al. (1987)by proposing a generalized single crossing property.Wilson (1993)derives first-order conditions for the optimality of a mechanism. Because this approach generally does not yield a description of the optimal mechanism, he also uses computational methods to obtain particular solutions.Rochet and Choné (1998)analyze a general multidimensional screening model and show that bunching will be a prominent feature of the optimal mechanism. In our model, the existing buyer’s private information is two-dimensional: his own valuation and the existence of the potential buyer. Because the second dimension is binary, we are able to obtain closed-form solutions. This paper is organized as follows. InSection 2, we provide the setup of the model. InSection 3, we present a benchmark model. InSection 4, we solve the seller’s optimization problem. InSection 5, we derive the optimal mechanism in several special cases and show that the conventional resale mechanism is suboptimal. InSection 6, we conclude. The appendix contains the technical proofs.
article.2. Model: A seller has one unit of indivisible object. There is an existing buyer A with private valuationvA. It is commonly known that with probabilityx∈(0,1)buyer A potentially knows buyer B whose private valuation isvB.6We assume that the seller can access buyer B only through buyer A’s referral, and the existence of buyer B is buyer A’s private information.7It is also commonly known thatvAandvBare independently drawn from atomless cumulative distribution functionsFA(vA)andFB(vB)with probability density functionsfA(vA)andfB(vB)on supports[a¯,a¯]and[b¯,b¯], respectively. FollowingMyerson (1981), we assume that bothFA(·)andFB(·)are regular, that is, their associated virtual value functionsJi(vi)≡vi−[1−Fi(vi)]/(fi(vi)),∀i∈{A,B},are increasing. We assume that the seller can verify whether buyer B indeed has a distributionFB(vB)through eligibility checks to rule out the possibility for buyer A to refer a random person.8The seller’s valuation is normalized to be zero, and all players are risk neutral. The mechanism design problem here is essentially a static one, with the complication that there is an ex ante unknown factor, the existence of buyer B, which needs to be resolved after the mechanism is proposed. Therefore, the mechanism has to be conditional on whether buyer B exists or not. The revelation principle inMyerson (1981)still applies, and it is without loss of generality to focus on direct mechanisms.9When buyer A reports that buyer B does not exist and his value isv˜A, the seller allocates the object to buyer A with probabilityqAN(v˜A)for an expected payment oftAN(v˜A). We refer to this submechanism asN−mechanism. When buyer A reports that buyer B exists and his value isv˜A, and buyer B reports that his value isv˜B, the seller assigns the object to buyer A and buyer B with probabilitiesqAE(v˜A,v˜B)andqBE(v˜A,v˜B)for expected paymentstAE(v˜A)andtBE(v˜B), respectively. We refer this submechanism asE−mechanism.10 Buyer A’s payoff by reportingv˜Aand nonexistence of B is given by Buyer A’s payoff by reportingv˜Aand existence of B is given by Buyer B’s payoff by reportingv˜Bis given by Incentive compatibility (IC) constraints and individual rationality (IR) constraints are defined as follows. A direct mechanism satisfies IC and IR if and only if (4) ensures that when B does not exist, A has no incentive to lie about his valuation. (5) ensures that when B exists, A has no incentive to lie about his valuation. (6) ensures that when B exists, A has no incentive to lie about B’s existence and his valuation simultaneously. In (6), although potentially buyer A can lie about his valuation, this is not profitable due to (4). Also, when B does not exist, A cannot report that B exists. (7) ensures that B has no incentive to lie about his valuation. IR Constraints (8)–(10) are standard and mean that participating in the mechanism is better than one’s outside option, which is normalized to be zero. Buyer A’s private information is two dimensional: valuation and knowledge about the existence of buyer B. DenoteUAN(vA)=uAN(vA,vA),UAE(vA)=uAE(vA,vA),UBE(vB)=uBE(vB,vB)as the truthful payoffs of the buyers. Given that all buyers are truthful, the seller’s expected revenue from a direct mechanism is The seller’s problem is choosing a direct mechanism to maximize the expected revenue subject to IC and IR constraints described inDefinition 1. Applying the envelope theorem on (4), (5), and (7) yields By similar arguments as inMyerson (1981)and using the previous equalities, we have a revenue equivalency result: The expected revenue for the seller is not affected by the detailed payment rules because they will be determined by the allocation rules. We thus can rewrite the original problem equivalently as (14) and (16) are standard, resulting from the second-order conditions of the IC constraints and IR constraints for the lowest valuations. (15) rewrites referral Constraint (6) by replacing expected payments with the allocation rules.
article.3. Benchmark: One important benchmark is when the seller observes whether buyer B exists or not and does not rely on buyer A’s referral. This is the same as when (15) does not appear. The following proposition characterizes the optimal mechanism in this benchmark model and shows that it is not feasible in the original problem. The proof is standard and thus omitted. In the benchmark model where the seller knows whether buyer B exists, the optimal mechanism is as follows: This mechanism violates(15)and thus is infeasible in the original problem. The solution to the benchmark is simple: The seller simply maximizes the expected revenue separately conditional on whether buyer B exists. InN−mechanism, the seller sells whenever buyer A’s virtual valuation is greater than zero. InE−mechanism, the seller sells to the buyer with the highest positive virtual valuations. It is also optimal to leave no informational rents to buyers with the lowest valuation regardless of whether buyer B exists. With this solution, unfortunately, buyer A is less likely to win in the presence of buyer B and thus has no incentive to refer buyer B, that is, the benchmark optimal mechanism violates referral Constraint (15) and is infeasible. This implies that to solve the original problem, we need to take (15) into consideration directly.
article.4. Original Problem: The following lemma characterizes some properties of the optimal mechanism in the original problem. In the optimal mechanism, we must haveUAN(a¯)=0,UBE(b¯)=0. As is common in the mechanism design literature and consistent with the benchmark model, the previous lemma shows that it is still optimal not to leave any informational rents to buyer A with the lowest valuation when buyer B does not exist and to buyer B with the lowest valuation. This lemma is straightforward because otherwise the seller can achieve a strictly higher revenue by reducing these information rents. However, we cannot concludeUAE(a¯)=0, because loweringUAE(a¯)could potentially violate the referral incentive (15). Note thatUAE(a¯)stands for the informational rent for buyer A with the lowest valuation when buyer B exists. This is the minimum amount of informational rent that has to be given out to buyer A when buyer B exists; we can call it a referral bonus. Obviously, the seller’s problem cannot be solved using the standard point-wise maximization directly due to referral Constraint (15). Our approach is as follows. We first obtain a relaxed problem by ignoring monotonicity Constraints (14) and relaxing (15). We then fully characterize the optimal mechanism in the relaxed problem and show that it also satisfies all the constraints in the original problem and thus is optimal in the original problem. The challenge is how to relax (15). The key observation is that buyer A with the highest valuation seems to have the least incentive to refer buyer B. Therefore, instead of imposing referral Constraint (15) for all valuations of buyer A, we only require it for the highest valuation. Readers with general interests are advised to skip the next section, which contains technical details for deriving the optimal mechanism and jump to the characterization of the optimal mechanism directly. 4.1. Relaxed Problem We thus obtain the following relaxed problem: The following lemma implies thatUAE(a¯)can be fully determined by allocation rules. Referral Constraint(21)must be binding: This is intuitive; otherwise, the designer can always achieve a higher revenue by either decreasingUAE(a¯)or moving the allocation closer to that for the benchmark. This implies that when buyer B exists, the seller always induces buyer A to make the referral. Therefore, we can replaceUAE(a¯)everywhere in the problem and now the seller only need to choose the allocation rules: We can take the continuous linear programming approach and construct the Lagrangian: The functionsvA+FA(vA)/fA(vA)andvAf(vA)are both increasing. This assumption is satisfied by many commonly studied distributions, such as uniform distributions, power distributions, and truncated normal and exponential distributions. The monotonicity condition onvA+FA(vA)/fA(vA), together with the regularity condition onFA(vA), implies the monotonicity ofJAN(vA;λ)andJAE(vA;λ). The regularity condition inFB(vB)implies the monotonicity ofJB(vB). These ensure the monotonicity of the allocation inE−mechanism. The monotonicity condition onvAfA(vA)ensures the monotonicity ofJAN(vA;λ)and the allocation monotonicity inN−mechanism. WhenAssumption 1fails, monotonicity in allocation cannot be obtained and standard ironing techniques inMyerson (1981)can be adopted to derive the optimal mechanism. Obviously, givenλandAssumption 1, the solution is as follows: For any givenλ, we can rewrite the left-hand side (LHS) of (24) in terms of the optimal allocation rule: Thus,G(λ)is the difference of buyer A’s winning probability between nonreferring and referring. Plugging (25)–(27) into (28), we can rewriteG(·)in terms of primitives: G(λ)is strictly increasing in[0,x]withG(x)>0. By Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions, the solution must haveλ=0orG(λ)=0. Therefore, ifG(0)≥0, then the optimalλisλ*=0; ifG(0)<0,Lemma 3implies that the optimalλis uniquely determined byG(λ*)=0.11The following example illustrates howλ*is determined. If both buyers’ valuations follow uniform distribution on [0, 1], then the corresponding virtual valuations are Plugging these into theGfunction, we obtain We summarize the optimal mechanism in the next section. 4.2. Optimal Mechanism and Its Properties Here we restate some key notations so that this section is self-contained. LetJAN(vA;λ)=JA(vA)−(x−λ)/(1−x)fA(vA)andJAE(vA;λ)=JA(vA)+(x−λ)/xfA(vA)be buyer A’s adjust virtual valuations inN−mechanismandE−mechanism, respectively. The following proposition summarizes the optimal solution in the relaxed problem and shows that it is also feasible in the original problem and thus solves the original problem. The optimal mechanism for the original problem is as follows: The optimal mechanism can be interpreted in a similar way as that for the benchmark model.12InN−mechanism, the seller should sell to buyer A whenever his adjusted virtual valuation is greater than zero; inE−mechanism, the seller allocates the object to the buyer with a higher positive adjusted virtual valuation. The nontrivial issue is how to adjust the virtual valuations. Compared with the benchmark, if a buyer’s virtue valuation is boosted (penalized), he will have a higher (lower) probability of winning the item and pay less (more) in the event of winning, which is akin to receiving carrots (sticks). Therefore, we have the following definition. A buyer receives virtue carrots (sticks) if his adjusted virtue valuation is higher (lower) than his virtue valuation. Buyer B’s virtual valuation is not adjusted. In contrast, buyer A’s virtual valuation is adjusted by−(x−λ*)/(1−x)fA(vA)inN−mechanismand by(x−λ*)/xfA(vA)inE−mechanism. Becauseλ*<x, we have the following. Compared with the benchmark model, buyer A receives strictly positive virtual sticks inN−mechanism,and strictly positive virtual carrots inE−mechanism. This is intuitive. Because of the existence of buyer B, the seller needs to distort the allocation rule to induce buyer A to refer buyer B. This can be achieved by either reducing the payoff for nonreferral inN−mechanismor increasing it for referral inE−mechanism. Furthermore, in the benchmark model, there is no need to provide a referral bonus; that is,UAEB(a¯)=0. The following corollary illustrates that it could be optimal to set the referral bonus strictly positive to provide referral incentives. Compared with the benchmark model, the seller needs to provide a strictly positive referral bonus, that is,UAE*(a¯)>0,if and only ifG(0)>0. The previous two corollaries fully spell out the difference between the optimal mechanism with referral and the benchmark model by revealing three different channels to provide incentive for buyer A to refer buyer B: virtual stick by penalizing the existing buyer’s virtual valuation inN−mechanism, virtual carrot by boosting the existing buyer’s virtual valuation inE−mechanism, and providing referral bonus.Corollary 1implies that it is necessary to distort the allocation rule, and the first two channels always arise.Corollary 2implies that the third channel may be needed. Thus, we conclude the following. To provide referral incentive, the carrots-and-sticks-via-virtual-value approaches are essential and providing referral bonus supplements them when necessary. This is intuitive. Compared with distorting allocation rules, providing referral bonuses is a pure monetary transfer and thus more costly for the seller. Indeed, the adjusted virtual valuations are indexed byλ*. It is always better to first change the allocation rule by reducingλwhile keeping zero referral bonus. Ifλhits zero and the corresponding mechanism still violates the referral constraint, the seller should provide a referral bonus. 4.3. Effect of Potential Buyer’s Existence and Strength It is of particular interest to investigate how the probability of the existence of buyer B would affect the optimal mechanism. This is summarized in the following proposition. When buyer B is more likely to exist, A lower referral bonus is needed. Buyer A receives more virtual sticks inN−mechanismand less virtual carrots inE−mechanism. Buyer A with any valuation is worse off. Here is the intuition. When buyer B is more likely to exist, the seller is less likely to sell to buyer A directly and will adopt more virtual sticks inN−mechanism. This reduces buyer A’s payoff from not referring buyer B. Therefore, there is less need to provide virtual carrots inE−mechanismor referral bonus. This explains the first two results. For the third result, it is because buyer B will be in direct competition with buyer A and the presence of buyer B hurts buyer A. Another interesting comparative static to perform involves the strength of buyer B. We can treat the virtual valuation of the buyer B,JB(vB), as a random variablewBwith CDFLB(wB). WhenLB(wB)increases in terms of first-order stochastic dominance, it becomes more likely that buyer B’s virtual valuation will lie above the seller’s valuezero, resulting in a sale, and it is intuitive to say that buyer B becomes stronger. Consider two possible value distributions for buyer B, denotedFB1(vB)andFB2(vB). Let the corresponding cumulative distributions of virtue valuations be represented byLB1andLB2.LB2is considered stronger thanLB1in terms of virtual valuation first-order stochastic dominance if, for allwB, we haveLB2(wB)≤LB1(wB).13We then have the following proposition. When buyer B becomes stronger in terms of virtual valuation first-order stochastic dominance, that is,LB2(wB)≤LB1(wB),∀wB, A higher referral bonus is needed. Buyer A receives more virtual sticks inN−mechanismand more virtual carrots inE−mechanism. Buyer A with the highest valuation is worse off, while buyer A with the lowest valuation is better off. When buyer B is stronger, the seller is less likely to sell to buyer A directly and will adopt more virtual sticks inN−mechanism. The not so intuitive parts are for the referral bonus and the distortion inE−mechanism. On one hand, less referral incentive is needed due to a lower payoff inN−mechanismfrom not referring. On the other hand, a stronger B makes buyer A worse off inE−mechanism. This reduces the payoff of buyer A from referring buyer B, which implies a greater need for referral incentives. It turns out that the impact onE−mechanismis of higher order, because buyer B does not appear inN−mechanismbut enters inE−mechanismdirectly. As a result, more referral incentives are needed overall. These explain the first two results. For the third result, a stronger buyer B has different implications for buyer A with different valuations. A stronger buyer B is more attractive to the seller, who is willing to provide a higher incentive for buyer A to refer; at the same time, a stronger B reduces buyer A’s chance of winning. The first effect benefits buyer A, whereas the second one causes harm. For buyer A with low valuation, winning is unlikely anyway and thus the first effect dominates. For buyer A with high valuation, winning is the priority, and the second effect dominates. The referral bonus can be interpreted as theexplicitreferral fee. Because the referral bonus is supplementary, it is interesting to ask when one would expect the seller to use it, which echoes with Daniel Grant’s question in the article. The answer is a direct implication ofPropositions 3and4. A higher explicit referral fee is needed when buyer B is less likely to exist and becomes stronger in terms of virtual valuation first-order stochastic dominance.
article.5. Implementation with Simple Mechanisms: In this section, we investigate when the optimal mechanism can be implemented by simple mechanisms. Just as standard auctions with reserve prices cannot implement Myerson’s optimal auction in general, we need further assumptions on primitives. Suppose that both buyers’ valuations are uniformly distributed on [0, 1]. According to (36), the optimal mechanism can be characterized as follows. Suppose both buyer A and buyer B’s valuations follow uniform distribution on[0, 1],then the optimal mechanism is The expected revenue is3x2+29x+48192. In this case, it is expected that one can implement the optimal mechanism by extending standard auctions. This is confirmed by the following proposition. With uniform symmetric buyers, the following modified second-price auction with conditional coupon implements the optimal mechanism. If buyer A does not refer buyer B, then set a take-it-or-leave-it price at1/2+x/8to buyer A. If buyer A refers buyer B, then run a second-price auction with reserve price12and conditional coupon equal to(1−x)/8to buyer A if buyer A wins. This mechanism is simple and easy to use because the conditional coupon is a constant and independent of bids. Compared with the benchmark, a higher price for nonreferral, that is,1/2+x/8>1/2, and a conditional coupon are used to distort the allocation as so to provide referral incentive. The conditional coupon can be thought of as a bonus when buyer A refers buyer B and wins in the auction. The reason why there is no need to provide informational rent for referral is because buyer A and buyer B are symmetric. In equilibrium, buyer A bidsvA+(1−x)/8and buyer B bidsvBin the auction. Finally, it is always better for buyer A to refer buyer B than accepting the take-it-or-leave-it offer directly. When buyer B is likely to have a much higher valuation than buyer A, it seems reasonable not to sell to buyer A when buyer A refers, and the optimal mechanism may be implementable by sequential selling. The following proposition provides such a situation. WhenJB(b¯)≥a¯+FA(a¯)/fA(a¯),the following sequential selling mechanism implements the optimal mechanism. First, make a take-it-or-leave-it price atJAN−1(0)to buyer A; if buyer A does not buy, then provide a cash bonus G(0)to refer buyer B and make a take-it-or-leave-it price atb¯to buyer B only. This mechanism is simple and easy to use because the referral bonus is a constant. This mechanism is a generalization of the mechanism adopted by Alice in the Introduction. 5.1. Would a Resale Mechanism Be Optimal? In our environment, one natural question is why does not the seller simply sell the object to buyer A, who then can resell the object to buyer B? Let us consider the following natural resale mechanism: the seller sets an optimal take-it-or-leave-it price to buyer A knowing that buyer A will set an optimal take-it-or-leave-it price to resell the item to buyer B if such a buyer exists.14 Let us start by assuming that both buyers’ valuations are uniformly distributed on [0, 1]. When buyer B exists, buyer A chooses the optimal resale pricer: Apparently, the optimal referral mechanism consistently generates higher expected revenue compared with both the optimal resale mechanism and the optimal mechanism by ignoring buyer B. The differences increase inx. The following proposition shows that the suboptimality of resale mechanism holds in general. The resale mechanism is suboptimal when referral is possible. Here is the intuition. In the resale mechanism, the seller does not communicate with buyer B directly. When buyer A communicates with buyer B, buyer A’s new knowledge about B becomes another piece of private information of buyer A and some extra information rent has to be provided. This proposition implies that whenever the seller has the opportunity to communicate directly with buyer B, it is always optimal to do so.
article.6. Conclusion: Although more buyers benefit the seller, it is not in the interest of a buyer to invite more buyers, as it reduces his chance of winning. This apparent conflict of interests makes the design of optimal mechanism nontrivial in providing referral incentive. In this paper, we provide a first step toward the design problem involved. We are able to identify three optimal channels. It is essential to distort the allocation using the carrots-and-sticks-via-virtual-value approach. When the potential buyer is less likely to exist and is stronger, the essential channels can no longer provide enough referral incentives. In this case, a supplementary channel is to provide a referral bonus. We also identify two situations in which the optimal mechanism can be implemented by simple mechanisms: either a second price auction with constant conditional coupon or sequential selling. Finally, we show that the conventional resale mechanism is suboptimal. There are many directions in which our analysis can be extended. For instance, we can assume that the existing buyer must incur a strictly positive cost to refer the potential buyer. In this case, we only need to compare the revenue difference between including and not including the potential buyer. Thus, the optimal mechanism is the better one of the two. It is compelling to extend the analysis to allow for general social networks, as is usually allowed in the literature with the axiomatic approach. Although it is possible to consider some specific network structures, a general treatment is challenging but rewarding. Finally, the referral incentive is relevant not only for auction design but also for any mechanism design problem. Our paper thus opens the possibility to consider an optimal referral incentive for a general mechanism design problem. These will be pursued by the authors in future work.
